stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) of integers modulo \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \), how many elements are units (that is, have a multiplicative inverse)?
solution: An element of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) is a unit exactly when it is coprime to \( [[nval]] \), so the number of units is \( \varphi([[nval]]) \). For \( n = p^{a} q^{b} \), \( \varphi(n) = p^{a-1}(p-1)\,q^{b-1}(q-1) = [[p]]^{[[a]]-1}([[p]]-1)\cdot[[q]]^{[[b]]-1}([[q]]-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts every nonzero residue as invertible, i.e. treats \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) as a field; but \( [[nval]] \) is composite, so only residues coprime to it are units.
This uses \( (p-1)(q-1) \), the totient of the squarefree number \( pq \); it drops the prime-power factors \( p^{a-1} \) and \( q^{b-1} \).
This uses \( (p^{a}-1)(q^{b}-1) \), an incorrect prime-power totient; the correct one is \( p^{a-1}(p-1) \), not \( p^{a}-1 \).
This counts the non-units (the zero divisors together with \( 0 \)) instead of the units.
This is one more than the correct count of units.
This is one fewer than the correct count of units.

stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), the element \( x = [[x]] \) is a unit. What is the multiplicative inverse of \( x \), i.e. the unique \( y \in \{1,\dots,[[nval]]-1\} \) with \( xy \equiv 1 \pmod{[[nval]]} \)?
solution: Since \( \gcd([[x]], [[nval]]) = 1 \), Euler's theorem gives \( x^{\varphi([[nval]])} \equiv 1 \pmod{[[nval]]} \), so \( x^{-1} \equiv x^{\varphi([[nval]]) - 1} \pmod{[[nval]]} \). Here \( \varphi([[nval]]) = [[phi_n]] \), and computing \( [[x]]^{[[phi_n]]-1} \bmod [[nval]] \) gives \( y = [[answer]] \); indeed \( [[x]] \cdot [[answer]] \equiv 1 \pmod{[[nval]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This is the additive complement \( n - x \), which satisfies \( x+y\equiv 0 \), not the multiplicative inverse \( xy\equiv 1 \).
This reduces the additive complement mod \( n \), still confusing the additive inverse with the multiplicative one.
This just repeats \( x \) itself, as if \( x \) were its own inverse.
This computes \( x^{2} \bmod n \) instead of the modular inverse.
This is one more than the correct inverse.
This is one fewer than the correct inverse.

stem: Let \( F = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), a field with \( [[p]] \) elements, and consider the polynomial ring \( F[x] \). Although \( F[x] \) is an infinite ring, it has only finitely many units, namely the nonzero constant polynomials. How many units does \( F[x] \) have?
solution: A nonzero polynomial \( p(x) \in F[x] \) is a unit exactly when its inverse is also a polynomial, which forces \( \deg p = 0 \), i.e. \( p(x) \) is a nonzero constant in \( F \). There are \( [[p]] \) constants total and \( 1 \) of them is \( 0 \), so there are \( [[p]] - 1 = [[answer]] \) units.
distractor_rationales: This counts \( 0 \) as a unit as well, but \( 0 \) is never invertible.
This is one more than \( [[p]] \), not related to removing the single non-unit \( 0 \) from the \( [[p]] \) constants.
This removes two elements instead of just the one non-unit \( 0 \).
This double-counts the units, as if \( \pm \) each nonzero constant gave a distinct unit.
This is two more than the correct count.
This is two fewer than the correct count.

stem: Consider the direct product ring \( R = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[q]]} \) (with \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \) distinct primes). An element \( (x,y) \in R \) is a unit exactly when both \( x \) and \( y \) are units in their own factors. How many units does \( R \) have?
solution: Since \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) and \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[q]]} \) are fields, each has \( [[p]]-1 \) and \( [[q]]-1 \) nonzero (hence unit) elements respectively. A pair \( (x,y) \) is a unit of the product exactly when \( x \ne 0 \) and \( y \ne 0 \), so the count is \( ([[p]]-1)([[q]]-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one less than \( |R| = pq \), as if only the zero pair \( (0,0) \) failed to be a unit; but any pair with EITHER coordinate zero is a non-unit.
This adds the two factor counts \( (p-1)+(q-1) \) instead of multiplying them; units of a product multiply, they don't add.
This is the full size \( |R| = pq \), not the count of units.
This adds instead of multiplying the per-factor unit counts.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one fewer than the correct count.
